title: The Question Detective
developmental_frame: At 11–12, thinking is mostly concrete but early abstract reasoning is appearing: a child can separate 'what I know' from 'what I'm guessing' if you give them the handle to do it. Fairness and rules feel enormous at this age, and they love catching an adult being wrong — that instinct is exactly the engine of good reasoning. Attention runs in shorter bursts than a teenager's, so loops must be quick and produce a visible result the same day.
authentic_value_meaning: At this age 'value' isn't money — it's being *trusted to figure something out*. Cracking a real question by yourself, and being able to show how you did it, is the reward. The audience is usually people they know (family, a friend, a teacher), and the win is 'you were right, and you can prove it'.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child picks a real question they personally want answered — is the '5-second rule' real, does our dog actually understand words, is the shortcut to school really faster — and gets to be the detective who settles it. The pull is the satisfaction of cracking it themselves plus the small thrill of proving something the grown-ups only assumed. No one has to make it fun; a genuine unanswered question they chose does that.

artifact: An 'investigation board' (paper or a simple slide) that states one real question, separates what was known / assumed / checked, shows the evidence gathered, and gives a verdict with the reasoning visible.
success_criteria: The child can point to the difference between a fact, a guess, and an opinion on their board.
They gathered at least one piece of real evidence themselves rather than just looking up an answer.
They can explain, out loud, why their verdict follows from their evidence.

lessons.title: Fact, guess, or opinion?
lessons.concept: Three different things get said in the same confident voice: a **fact** (checkably true), a **guess** (might be true, not checked yet), and an **opinion** (how someone feels). Smart thinking starts with telling them apart.
lessons.real_example: 'It rained here yesterday' is a fact you can check. 'It'll rain tomorrow' is a guess. 'Rainy days are the best' is an opinion. Same tone of voice — very different things.
lessons.apply_task: Grab five statements from a cereal box, an advert, or a family conversation. Label each one fact, guess, or opinion. The tricky ones are the point.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which was hardest to label? Sneaky statements often dress an opinion up as a fact — did you catch any?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your five labels to a parent or friend. Where you two disagree, talk out why — the disagreement usually reveals the sneaky one.
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach a younger child the three types using their own toys or snacks as examples. If they can label three new statements right, you nailed the teaching.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is a *no-AI* lesson on purpose. The skill is your own ear catching the difference — outsourcing that to a chatbot defeats it. Later, once you can label reliably, AI can be a sparring partner to check yourself.
success_criteria: Five statements are labelled with at least one 'sneaky' one identified.

lessons.title: A question you can actually answer
lessons.concept: Big questions like 'are dogs smart?' can't be settled. Good detectives shrink a big question into a small, checkable one — 'does my dog come to the word *walk* even when I say it flat, with no excited voice?'
lessons.real_example: 'Is the school shortcut faster?' is too vague. 'Is the alley route faster than the main road when I walk normally?' can be timed and settled this week.
lessons.apply_task: Take your big curious question and rewrite it as one small question you could actually check in a few days. Write both versions so you can see the shrink.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did shrinking the question change what you'd need to look at? Smaller questions usually point straight at the evidence you need.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask an adult: 'If I checked this, would it really answer my big question?' Adjust if they poke a hole.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a friend shrink one of *their* big questions into a checkable one.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Fine to ask AI 'help me make this question more specific' — it's good at that. But you decide if the smaller question still matches what you actually wanted to know; the model can't feel your curiosity for you.
success_criteria: One checkable small question is written that clearly connects to the bigger curiosity.

lessons.title: Go get the evidence
lessons.concept: Evidence is proof you gather *yourself*: a measurement, a count, a test, a careful look. Looking up someone else's answer isn't evidence — it's borrowing a verdict.
lessons.real_example: To test the alley shortcut, you don't Google it — you walk both routes with a timer, three times each, and write down the numbers. Now you *have* proof.
lessons.apply_task: Run your check at least three times and write down what happened each time. Three tries, because one could be a fluke.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did every try give the same result? If not, what might explain the difference — and does it change your answer?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your raw results to someone and ask: 'Would you believe this? Is three tries enough?' Note their answer.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why doing the test three times beats doing it once.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Do NOT ask AI for the answer to your question — that skips the whole job. AI can help you *design* a fair test ('how do I make this timing fair?'), but the evidence has to come from you doing it.
assets_needed: Evidence log (3+ rows for repeated tries)
success_criteria: At least three real observations were gathered and recorded by the child.

lessons.title: Show your verdict — and your working
lessons.concept: A detective doesn't just announce 'guilty'. They walk you through the clues so you can see it too. Your verdict is only as strong as the reasoning you can show.
lessons.real_example: 'The alley is faster — here are my six times, the alley averaged 4 minutes, the main road 6. That's my proof.' Anyone can now check your thinking, not just trust your word.
lessons.apply_task: Finish your investigation board: the question, what you knew vs assumed, your evidence, and the verdict with the reasoning laid out.
lessons.reflection_prompt: If someone disagreed with your verdict, which part of your board would they attack? Is that part solid?
lessons.feedback_task: Present the board to two people. Ask each to try to poke a hole. Fix the weakest spot they find.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk a family member through your whole investigation as if teaching them to be a detective — question, evidence, verdict.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you tidy how the board *reads*, but the reasoning must be yours and you must be able to defend every step without it. If you can't explain a line on your board yourself, take it off.
assets_needed: Investigation board template (question / known / assumed / evidence / verdict)
success_criteria: A completed board shows the reasoning, not just the answer.
The child defended their verdict against at least one challenge.
